caption.text:
7,570 meters. The highest point on Earth that no human has ever touched.

Gangkhar Puensum sits on the disputed border between Bhutan and Tibet. Its name means 'White Peak of the Three Spiritual Brothers' — and to the communities of Laya and Lunana, those three summits are not geography. They are the residences of yul lha, local deities who govern weather, harvests, and the fate of the valleys below. Disturbing them is believed to invite storms, crop failure, and disease.

Between 1983 and 1994, when Bhutan briefly allowed mountaineering, four expeditions tried. A Japanese team retreated at 6,850m. An Austrian expedition endured 21 straight days of avalanches. A British team led by Steve Berry reached 6,850m before jet-stream winds forced a helicopter evacuation. None made it.

In 1994, Bhutan banned climbing above 6,000m. In 2003, all mountaineering was prohibited. The reason: respect for spiritual beliefs, and the absence of high-altitude rescue infrastructure. In a country that measures success by Gross National Happiness, some peaks are not meant to be conquered.

You can trek to base camp. You can see all three summits from Kilkor Thang. But you cannot climb. And maybe that's the point.

Would you rather stand on the summit, or leave something sacred untouched?

text.line_2: Not for danger. For the gods.

text.line_1: Some places are not meant to be conquered.
